text: The surgeon excises and mobilizes the colostomy, detaching it completely from the abdominal wall. The anvil of the circular stapler is then introduced and secured in the colon using a hand-sewn pursing suture.
The surgeon repositions the colon within the peritoneal cavity, ensuring proper alignment for the ileorectal anastomosis procedure.

The surgeon utilizes the colostomy site as an access point for the single port device, inserting one trocar for the camera and two working trocars. Pneumoperitoneum is established at a pressure of twelve millimeters of mercury.
The surgeon utilizes standard laparoscopic instruments along with an intracorporeal articulating sealing device, visible within the surgical field.
The surgeon stands between the patient's legs to mobilize the flexure, with surgical monitors positioned centrally for optimal viewing.

With the patient tilted to the right side, the surgeon performs adhesiolysis and mobilizes the descending colon and splenic flexure from lateral to medial, ensuring sufficient mobility of the left colon.
The surgeon and assistant stand at the right side of the patient, preparing to perform the rectal portion of the ileorectal anastomosis procedure.
The surgeon detaches loops of the small bowel located in the pelvis to uncage the rectal sigmoidal stump, using laparoscopic instruments visible through the single port device.
The surgeon mobilizes and isolates the rectal sigmoidal stump, ensuring it is free from surrounding tissues and prepared for the next stage of the ileorectal anastomosis.
The surgeon advances the circular stapler into the recto sigmoidal stump, preparing for the anastomosis.
The surgeon performs a tension-free ileorectal anastomosis by connecting the anvil with the circular stapler introduced transanally, ensuring proper alignment and secure attachment of the bowel ends.
The surgeon tests the ileorectal anastomosis by injecting saline and air, observing for any leaks or defects at the surgical site.
The surgeon uses absorbable sutures to close the ostomy site, followed by closing the skin with a pursed ring suture using non-absorbable material.
